setting.location: Meridian Space Center, Mission Control Building 7, Houston, Texas
setting.environment: A sealed, windowless mission control floor humming with mainframe computers, telemetry boards, and blinking console lights. A fierce Gulf Coast storm — Hurricane Inez's outer bands — has knocked out external roads and grounded all aircraft, trapping the skeleton night crew inside the facility under mandatory lockdown protocol until the storm passes and the range safety officer clears departure.

crime.what_happened: Senior Flight Dynamics Officer Gerald Hoyt was found dead at his console, an apparent cardiac arrest. However, the automated coffee dispenser beside his station was found to have been laced with a fast-acting digitalis compound, and critical trajectory data tapes for the upcoming Apollo test mission were simultaneously erased — an act of deliberate sabotage that would have caused a rocket misfire and covered the murder by making Hoyt's death look stress-related.
crime.when: Between 02:15 and 02:50 on the night of October 14, 1967, during the storm-induced blackout period when backup generators took four minutes to restore partial lighting.
crime.initial_discovery: Communications Technician Rosa Delgado returned from the equipment closet at 02:51 and found Hoyt slumped over his console, his coffee cup overturned. She raised the alarm and facility security sealed all exits under standing emergency protocol.

characters.name: Gerald Hoyt
characters.occupation: Senior Flight Dynamics Officer responsible for trajectory verification on all manned test missions
characters.alibi: Was seated at Console 4 working alone on trajectory recalculations when the blackout occurred; Rosa Delgado saw him alive at 02:15 before leaving for the equipment closet.
characters.secret: Hoyt had recently discovered that classified trajectory data was being copied and passed to a foreign contact, and had written a formal report naming a colleague — a report locked in his desk drawer that he had not yet submitted to his supervisor.

characters.name: Dr. Nadia Voss
characters.occupation: Systems Integration Engineer, embedded in Mission Control to oversee computer mainframe upgrades under a government contract
characters.motive: Voss is a Soviet-born émigré who was coerced by KGB handlers threatening her elderly mother still living in Leningrad; she had been feeding trajectory data to a Soviet contact for eight months and knew Hoyt had identified her. Eliminating Hoyt and destroying the data tapes would erase all evidence of her espionage.
characters.alibi: Claims she was in the mainframe room on Level B performing routine tape backups during the blackout, but no one can independently confirm her presence there for the full 35-minute window.
characters.secret: Voss had obtained a key to the mainframe room's secondary access corridor — a key that also opens the service panel behind Hoyt's console — by charming the facilities manager under false pretenses six weeks earlier. She has kept this key hidden in the lining of her badge lanyard.

characters.name: Warren Stubbs
characters.occupation: Flight Surgeon assigned to monitor crew health data during simulations and night shifts
characters.motive: Stubbs married a wealthy widow eighteen months ago under a false identity, concealing a prior marriage that was never dissolved. Hoyt had recognized Stubbs from a newspaper photograph connected to a bigamy investigation in another state and had quietly confronted him the previous week, threatening to report him to NASA security unless Stubbs resigned. Stubbs stood to lose his career, his security clearance, and his access to his wife's considerable inheritance.
characters.alibi: Claims he was in the medical bay on the east corridor reviewing crew biometric logs from 02:00 onward, with the logs themselves as the only supporting record — no other person was present.
characters.secret: Stubbs has access to the facility's pharmaceutical cabinet, which contains digitalis preparations used for cardiac stress testing. A vial of digitalis solution was logged out under a test protocol number Stubbs himself filed three days before the murder, but the protocol has no corresponding experiment on record.

characters.name: Colonel Ray Ashford
characters.occupation: NASA Liaison Officer from the Department of Defense, stationed in Mission Control to oversee classified payload specifications on the upcoming test mission
characters.motive: Ashford is under secret investigation by the Inspector General for diverting DoD funds to a private aerospace consulting firm he controls through a proxy. Hoyt had inadvertently received a misdirected internal memo outlining Ashford's financial irregularities and had mentioned it to Ashford directly, not yet understanding its significance. Ashford feared Hoyt would eventually connect the dots and had strong reason to silence him before the IG inquiry concluded.
characters.alibi: States he was in the secure communications room on Level A sending encrypted status reports during the blackout, a room that requires a two-person sign-in but whose log shows only his own entry — the second officer who should have co-signed was elsewhere during the storm.
characters.secret: Ashford carries a personal override badge that grants him access to all consoles on the mission control floor, including the ability to unlock and erase data tapes without triggering the standard audit trail. He received this badge through an informal arrangement and its existence is not listed on any official personnel document.

characters.name: Rosa Delgado
characters.occupation: Communications Technician responsible for maintaining telemetry relay equipment and inter-console cabling
characters.alibi: Was in the equipment closet on the north wall from 02:18 to 02:51, replacing a faulty relay module; the replaced module is logged with a timestamp and she left the old part on the shelf as per protocol.
characters.secret: Rosa saw Dr. Voss exit the service corridor beside Console 4 at approximately 02:22 — well before the backup generators restored light — but said nothing initially because Voss had told her earlier that evening she might need to access a cable junction behind that console, and Rosa assumed it was routine. She did not realize the significance until security began asking questions.

evidence.name: Digitalis Vial Log Entry
evidence.description: A pharmaceutical checkout ledger from the medical bay cabinet shows that a 10ml vial of digitalis solution was signed out three days before the murder under a stress-test protocol number filed by Warren Stubbs. When investigators cross-reference the protocol number against the experiment scheduling database, no corresponding test is on record, suggesting the checkout was fabricated to obtain the poison.

evidence.name: Hidden Secondary Access Key
evidence.description: A small brass key was found sewn into the fabric lining of Dr. Nadia Voss's badge lanyard when security conducted a personal effects search. Facilities management confirms the key matches the lock for the service corridor panel directly behind Console 4, and the corridor provides direct access to both Hoyt's station and the data tape storage rack — allowing someone to move between these points without crossing the open floor.

evidence.name: Overturned Coffee Cup with Residue
evidence.description: Hoyt's standard-issue NASA coffee cup was found overturned on Console 4, with a small pool of coffee visible on the console surface. A residue sample taken by the facility nurse shows traces of a glycoside compound consistent with digitalis; the cup came from the automated dispenser unit bolted to the wall beside the console, suggesting the poison was introduced into the dispenser's individual serving mechanism rather than the main reservoir.

evidence.name: Erased Data Tape Reel
evidence.description: Tape reel T-44, containing the verified trajectory calculations for the upcoming Apollo test mission, was found blank in its storage rack — a complete bulk erasure that should require either a degaussing tool or the override badge. The reel's chain-of-custody tag shows it was last accessed at 02:33, during the blackout window, but the standard console audit log shows no access event, indicating the override badge documented in Colonel Ashford's possession was used to bypass logging.

evidence.name: Hoyt's Unsubmitted Report
evidence.description: A hand-typed three-page document was found locked in the top drawer of Hoyt's desk, addressed to the NASA Security Division and dated two days before his death. The report details a pattern of unauthorized data tape access linked to the mainframe room secondary corridor, names Dr. Nadia Voss as the primary suspect, and references specific dates when classified trajectory data appeared to have been copied — providing a clear motive for Voss to silence Hoyt before he delivered the report.

evidence.name: Storm Drain Access Footprint
evidence.description: A single muddy boot print was found inside the service corridor near the access panel behind Console 4, consistent in size with a women's size 7 work boot. The mud composition contains a distinctive mixture of Gulf clay and machine oil found only near the exterior drainage grate on the north side of the building — suggesting someone entered or passed through the corridor from an external-adjacent utility path during the storm.

evidence.name: Faulty Relay Module Timestamp
evidence.description: The replaced relay module left by Rosa Delgado in the equipment closet carries an automatic timing stamp from the console diagnostic system showing it went offline at 02:17 and was reconnected at 02:49, fully corroborating her account of her whereabouts. This evidence clears Rosa as a suspect but also confirms that for the entire blackout window, the north corridor was unmonitored and the service panel behind Console 4 was accessible.

evidence.name: Power Surge Report — Mainframe Room
evidence.description: A routine system log from the mainframe room on Level B shows an uninterrupted sequence of automated tape backup operations from 02:00 to 03:30, with no manual interaction events recorded during the blackout window. This directly contradicts Dr. Voss's alibi claim that she was performing hands-on tape backups in that room, as any manual action would have registered in the log.

evidence.name: Raccoon Intrusion Report from Security Guard
evidence.description: Night security guard Pete Mallow filed a written incident report at 02:05 noting that the exterior motion sensors on the east perimeter had been triggered, and that upon inspection he found a large raccoon had disturbed a trash receptacle near the east entrance. The report was initially circulated among the investigation team as a possible explanation for why the motion sensors did not capture human movement during the blackout window — however, the east entrance is on the opposite side of the building from the service corridor access point, making the raccoon entirely irrelevant to the crime scene.

evidence.name: Rosa Delgado's Witness Statement
evidence.description: In her formal written statement taken by the facility security officer at 04:10, Rosa Delgado describes seeing a figure exit the service corridor door beside Console 4 at approximately 02:22, during the blackout. She identifies the figure by a distinctive white streak in the hair and the NASA contractor badge lanyard, both of which match Dr. Nadia Voss's appearance — placing Voss at the scene of the crime during the time of death.

solution.method: Voss used the secretly obtained service corridor key to access the panel behind Console 4 undetected during the four-minute blackout. She had pre-loaded digitalis solution — obtained separately, not through the medical bay — into the individual serving capsule of Hoyt's assigned dispenser port earlier in the shift when she passed his station. During the blackout she erased tape reel T-44 using the service corridor access to the tape storage rack adjacent to the console, then slipped back to the Level B mainframe room before lights were restored. The automated tape backup log running in her absence created the appearance of an alibi, which she believed would be impenetrable.
solution.motive: Voss was being coerced by Soviet intelligence handlers who threatened her mother in Leningrad; she had fed classified trajectory data to a contact for eight months. Hoyt had independently discovered the unauthorized access pattern and written a report naming her. Eliminating Hoyt and destroying the evidence tapes was her only path to avoiding exposure, arrest, and the destruction of her family in the USSR.
solution.how_to_deduce: Step 1 — Establish that the murder was premeditated and internal: E3 confirms digitalis in Hoyt's cup, and E1 shows Stubbs obtained a digitalis vial under a false protocol, making him the initial prime suspect. However, E1 only establishes access to one poison supply; Voss could have obtained digitalis independently, and E1 does not place Stubbs at the scene. Step 2 — Eliminate the false suspects: Stubbs's medical bay logs (E1 cross-reference) show the vial he checked out was never returned and never used in any documented test, which is suspicious but circumstantial. Ashford's override badge (E4) explains the tape erasure but not the poisoning, and nothing places him at Console 4. The raccoon report (E9) initially diverts attention to the east entrance security gap, but the service corridor entry point is on the north side, making E9 irrelevant and a deliberate distraction. Step 3 — Focus on the service corridor: E6 places a size-7 boot print inside the service corridor, and E2 proves Voss possessed the only unofficial key to that corridor's access panel. No other character had this access. Step 4 — Destroy the alibi: E8 shows the mainframe room log recorded only automated operations during the blackout — no manual interaction — directly disproving Voss's claim of hands-on tape backups. She was not where she said she was. Step 5 — Place Voss at the scene: E10 has Rosa Delgado identifying Voss by her distinctive hair and lanyard exiting the service corridor at 02:22, during the window of death. Combined with E5 — Hoyt's unsubmitted report naming Voss as his espionage suspect — motive, opportunity, means, and placement at the scene all converge exclusively on Dr. Nadia Voss.

key_twists: Stubbs's fabricated pharmaceutical log makes him appear to be the poisoner for most of the game, as digitalis is the murder weapon and he has documented access to it — only careful cross-referencing with the mainframe log and witness statement breaks the misdirection.
The raccoon security report (E9) is a physical red herring that focuses early investigation on the wrong side of the building and the wrong access point, consuming considerable player attention.
Voss's espionage motive only fully emerges when players find Hoyt's locked report (E5) — until that point, her motive is invisible and she appears to be a cooperative witness.

Colonel Ashford's override badge explains the tape erasure but not the murder, leading players to briefly suspect a two-person conspiracy before the service corridor evidence unifies both acts under a single culprit.
The automated mainframe log that Voss believed would protect her is the very evidence that destroys her alibi — her technological sophistication becomes her undoing.

_meta.setting_input: Sabotage at a 1960s NASA mission control centre
